coredata.dc:title: Hardware-in-the-Loop simulation for online identification of lithium-ion battery model parameters and state of charge estimation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Le, Quoc Dan
Lam, Quoc Minh
Huynh, Minh Nhat
Nguyen, Huy Hung
Duong, Van Tu
coredata.dc:description:
Accurately estimating the state of charge (SOC) of Lithium batteries remains a major challenge due to their nonlinear behavior, especially when the initial SOC value significantly deviates from the actual one. To address this issue, this study introduces a new algorithm to estimate the battery SOC based on adaptive unscented Kalman filter (AUKF). The modified AUKF (mAUKF) begins by constructing a second-order Thevenin model of the batteries through the forgetting factor recursive least squares (FFRLS) method, and then implements the novel approach for SOC estimation. The proposed method retains the strengths of the AUKF while dynamically adjusting the process and measurement noise covariance to improve SOC estimation robustness and accuracy. The effectiveness of the method is validated using both simulation and experimental results. Additionally, to avoid the limitations of pure simulation or experimentation in validating when there are novel SOC estimation algorithms, a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test bench is also introduced. The final results demonstrate that the mAUKF algorithm achieves faster convergence rates of about
45 %
and
49 %
when handling large initial errors and transient responses compared to the original methods, in simulation and experiment cases, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: State-of-charge
Adaptive unscented Kalman filter
Hardware-in-the-loop
Forgetting factor recursive least squares
battery management systems

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Digital Control and System Engineering

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Viet Nam National University Ho Chi Minh City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is funded by Vietnam National University Ho Chi Minh City (VNU-HCM) under grant number B2023\u201320\u201302. We acknowledge the support of time and facilities from Key Laboratory of Digital Control and System Engineering (DCSELab), Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology (HCMUT), VNU-HCM for this study.

This research is funded by Vietnam National University Ho Chi Minh City (VNU-HCM) under grant number B2023-20-02 . We acknowledge the support of time and facilities from Key Laboratory of Digital Control and System Engineering (DCSELab), Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology (HCMUT), VNU-HCM for this study.

row.entry: Integrates the current over time to estimate the State of Charge (SOC).
Simple and easy to implement in real-time.
Accumulated errors due to noise, sensor drift, and dependence on an accurate initial SOC value.
Based on the SOCOCV relationship, requiring a long rest time for voltage stabilization.
Requires long resting times, nonlinear behavior in the SOC range 20 %−90 %, unsuitable for real-time use.
Combines CC with additional algorithms such as error correction, OCV updates, or dynamic compensation to improve accuracy.
Reduces accumulated errors and improves accuracy.
Still depends on an accurate initial SOC value and requires recalibration.
Handles the nonlinear battery system using linear approximation of the state-space model.
Handles nonlinearity and is accurate when the model is well-defined.
Errors from linear approximation, high computational requirements, and requires Jacobian matrix.
Uses Unscented Transform to improve accuracy in nonlinear systems without requiring linearization.
More accurate than EKF and does not require Jacobian matrix computation.
Assuming process and measurement noise remain constant; computational cost is higher than EKF.
Dynamically updates the noise covariance matrices during SOC estimation.
Improves accuracy under changing noise conditions and adapts well to dynamic environments.
Slow convergence when the initial SOC error is large; high computational cost.

entry.bold: The modified Adaptive Unscented Kalman Filter (mAUKF)

row.entry: Improves AUKF by using an exponential weighting method to dynamically adjust noise matrices, enhancing convergence speed under large initial errors.
Good adaptability and faster convergence with large initial errors.
Requires parameter tuning for weighting factors and is more complex than the standard AUKF.
Uses machine learning algorithms to model the nonlinear behavior of SOC based on historical data.
High accuracy and flexibility for complex nonlinear systems.
Requires large datasets, significant retraining time when battery types change.
Combines multiple methods (e.g., CC + EKF or NN + EKF) to leverage the advantages of each method and address limitations.
Combines the strengths of different methods to achieve high accuracy.
Complex implementation and requires tuning of many hybrid parameters.
Applies advanced machine learning algorithms such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), Deep Neural Networks (DNN), and fuzzy logic to predict SOC.
Accurate in complex conditions and highly adaptable.
High computational cost and dependency on the quality of input datasets.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The pseudo code of mAUKF to estimate SOC of battery.

list-item.para:
Initialize:
-
Measure terminal voltage in static state;
-
Compute initial
S O C ( 0 )
using OCV-SOC function;
-
Estimate initial model parameters:
R 0
( 0 )
,
R 1
( 0 )
,
R 2
( 0 )
,
C 1
( 0 )
,
C 2
( 0 )
.
Compute initial coefficients from model parameters.

Apply mAUKF to estimate the current
S O C
.

Calculate open-circuit voltage (
U
O C
) using the OCV-SOC function.
Update model parameters using FFRLS based on current measurements.
Modify coefficients with updated model parameters.

Re-apply AUKF to estimate
S O C
for the next step.

8. Repeat steps 4–7 iteratively for continuous
S O C
estimation and parameter updates.

Performance of various estimated methods in terms of RSME and MAE during pure simulation.
Comparison of SOC estimation methods under different initial covariance estimates during pure simulation.
Comparison of SOC estimation methods under different initial covariance estimates during experiment.
Comparison of SOC estimation methods under different initial covariance estimates during HIL simulation.
Comparison of SOC estimation methods under different initial covariance estimates for 29 cells in simulation.

head.ce:title: Hardware-in-the-Loop simulation for online identification of lithium-ion battery model parameters and state of charge estimation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Accurately estimating the state of charge (SOC) of Lithium batteries remains a major challenge due to their nonlinear behavior, especially when the initial SOC value significantly deviates from the actual one. To address this issue, this study introduces a new algorithm to estimate the battery SOC based on adaptive unscented Kalman filter (AUKF). The modified AUKF (mAUKF) begins by constructing a second-order Thevenin model of the batteries through the forgetting factor recursive least squares (FFRLS) method, and then implements the novel approach for SOC estimation. The proposed method retains the strengths of the AUKF while dynamically adjusting the process and measurement noise covariance to improve SOC estimation robustness and accuracy. The effectiveness of the method is validated using both simulation and experimental results. Additionally, to avoid the limitations of pure simulation or experimentation in validating when there are novel SOC estimation algorithms, a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test bench is also introduced. The final results demonstrate that the mAUKF algorithm achieves faster convergence rates of about
45 %
and
49 %
when handling large initial errors and transient responses compared to the original methods, in simulation and experiment cases, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries have been widely used in energy storage systems and electrified transportation, especially in electric vehicles and unmanned vehicle applications. In these systems, batteries not only power devices but also support grid stability through bidirectional charging and discharging, because of their high energy density, reliable performance, and long cycle life [1–3],. However, their fragile nature requires a battery management system (BMS) capable of monitoring parameters and determining operating states to ensure safe and efficient operation. The state of charge (SOC) of battery is a key feature of the BMS because it represents the remaining capacity of the battery. Besides, the SOC can help BMS determine which control action to take such as charging, discharging, or balancing the battery [4,5],. As a result, the accurate estimation of the battery's SOC during operating plays an important role in improving both their safety, efficiency, and life cycle during operation. However, accurately estimating SOC remains a significant challenge because SOC is an interdependent characteristic that can only be indirectly determined through other parameters [6].

Traditional methods such as coulomb counting (CC) and open-circuit voltage (OCV) are foundational in defining SOC. The CC method integrates current over time for real-time SOC estimates, requiring an accurate initial SOC value for reliable result. However, this method accumulates noise and errors, which inevitably leads to drift in the estimation. The OCV method estimates the SOC through SOCOCV correlation, but a long relaxation time is obligatory for accurately estimating. Particularly, with Li-ion batteries, the OCV remains almost constant when the SOC varies between 20 % and 90 %. These limitations restrict the applicability of these methods from high-end commercial products such as electric vehicles (EVs) [7]. Numerous enhanced coulomb counting (ECC) methods are developed in order to address the inaccuracy of CC and OCV. These methods combine the original CC method with other algorithms such as error correction, dynamic compensation, and hybrid integrations with additional measurements or models. However, although ECC methods reduce the cumulative error by updating the initial value of SOC or noise filtering, these still depend on accurate initial SOC to reliably estimate. Consequently, ECC methods struggle to accurately estimate SOC where there is large initial SOC error [8–11].

Recently, model-based approaches have attracted interest from researchers as solutions to improve estimation accuracy and reduce the dependency on an accurate initial SOC value. These methods depend in a precise open-circuit model with the OCV-SOC relationship, including the extended Kalman filter (EKF), unscented Kalman filter (UKF), or AUKF. By leveraging mathematical models of battery dynamics, these methods are capable of handling nonlinearities in battery behavior. The UKF, in particular, handles nonlinearities better than the EKF by avoiding linearization. However, both these approaches operate assuming that the process noise and measurement noise remain constant, which limits their ability to adapt to the dynamic conditions of real-world environment. The AUKF addresses this limitation by dynamically adapting the process and measurement noise covariances during estimation. This capability significantly enhances SOC estimation accuracy, even in varying operating environment [12–16]. However, the AUKF still requires multiple cycles to accurately estimate SOC when the initial value deviates significantly, especially after prolonged signal loss and subsequent system reconnection.

As computing technology continues to progress, neural networks and machine learning-based methods are also being researched in order to attain higher accuracy SOC. These data-driven methods are highly flexible and can provide highly accurate estimation value without the requirement of mathematical model. However, these require extensive datasets, which are often time-consuming to collect, and the accuracy of these methods is also greatly influenced by the quality of this data. As a consequence, when new batteries with different characteristics are created, these models need to be retrained, resulting in significant computational overhead and data collection challenges. This dependency on datasets limits their practicality for real-time applications, especially when transitioning between various battery types or configurations [17–21].

Aiming to effectively handle large initial error after prolonged signal loss in estimating SOC without the need of large datasets, this study introduces a novel SOC estimation method based on the AUKF framework. The mAUKF algorithm enhances the traditional AUKF by employing an exponential weighting method to dynamically adjust process and measurement noise covariance, enabling robust SOC estimation under significant initialization errors. Additionally, a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test bench is also introduced to validate the proposed method in real-time, ensuring practical feasibility under dynamic operating conditions. This test bench ensures practical evaluation under dynamic and realistic conditions, overcoming the limitations of conventional validation approaches. To clarify the contribution of this study, a summary of the limitations of previous works and the novelty of the proposed method will be provided in Table 1 .

Typically, validating SOC estimation algorithms is performed through simulation-based methods or experimental test benches. Simulation provides a cost-effective and flexible platform for testing algorithms under ideal conditions using mathematical models of battery behavior. However, it cannot fully replicate real-world challenges, such as noise, sensor inaccuracies, and environmental variations [22,23],. On the other hand, experimental test benches offer realistic data but often require significant resources, including complex setups and high costs, which limit their practicality in many research facilities [24–26]. The HIL approach combines the benefits of both simulation and real-world experimentation by integrating software-based battery emulators with physical BMS. This allows for real-time validation of SOC estimation algorithms under dynamic and realistic operating conditions while ensuring safety, consistency, and cost-effectiveness. By using the HIL test bench, the performance and robustness of the proposed mAUKF algorithm are validated efficiently in practical scenarios.

The structure of this paper is as follows: Section II introduces the theoretical foundation of the battery model, including the state-space formulation and model parameter identification. Section III provides the design and development of the mAUKF algorithm for SOC estimation. Section IV presents simulation-based validation of the proposed algorithm. In Section V, the experimental setup utilizing the HIL test bench is described, and results demonstrating the algorithm's accuracy and robustness are provided. Finally, Section VI summarizes the key findings and outlines future research directions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Online identification of lithium-ion battery model parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
First, the equivalent circuit model (ECM) of the battery is introduced. The selection of an appropriate ECM depends on the required accuracy, computational complexity, and specific application. In practice, the second-order RC model of a battery is commonly chosen because it strikes a balance between accuracy compared to simpler models (such as the pure resistor model and first-order RC model [27]) and computational cost, which is significantly lower than that of higher-order or hybrid methods [28]. The ECM of the battery is shown in Fig. 1 , where
R 0
represents the internal resistance of the battery;
R 1
and
R 2
are the polarization resistances;
C 1
and
C 2
are the polarization capacitances;
U 0
denotes the voltage drop across the internal resistance,
U 1
and
U 2
are the corresponding polarization voltages;
U
O C
is the open circuit voltage of the battery;
U T
is the terminal voltage of the battery;
I B
is the discharging or charging current.

Through Kirchhoff's law, one can compute the terminal voltage
U T
in the following relationship:
(1)
U
O C
−
U T
=
U 1
+
U 2
+
R 0
×
I B
.

Applying Ohm's law for C1 and C2 capacitors, it can be obtained as follows:
(2)
{
U ˙
1
= −
U 1
R 1
C 1
+
I B
C 1
,
U ˙
2
= −
U 2
R 2
C 2
+
I B
C 2
.

By using Eq. (2) and taking the Laplace transformation of Eqs. (1) and (2), it yields
(3)
U
O C
( s )

( s )
=

( s )

+
R 1

s + 1
+

+ 1
)
.

Defining the transfer function
G ( s )
as the ratio of the output to the input, it results in
(4)
G
( s )
Δ
=
U
O C
( s )

U T
( s )

( s )

R 0

where
τ 1

=

and

are respectively the time constants of
R 1
-
C 1
and
R 2
-
C 2
networks.

On the other hand, the FFRLS algorithm is selected for identifying battery model parameters due to its fast adaptability and low computational complexity. Batteries are nonlinear systems with time-varying parameters influenced by aging, temperature, and load current. FFRLS employs a forgetting factor to downweighs previously sampled data while emphasizing next state, enabling the algorithm to effectively track dynamic changes in battery parameters [29]. Additionally, FFRLS has a computational complexity of
O (
n
k
2
)
, which is significantly lower compared to methods like the Kalman Filter or Particle Filter [30]. The algorithm requires storing only an inverse matrix and a parameter vector, rather than the entire historical dataset, thereby reducing memory usage. As a result, FFRLS is particularly well-suited for embedded systems or devices with limited computational resources, such as BMS. A FFRLS model is employed to identify system parameters in the following recursive form:
(5)

=
a 1

,
a 2

a 5
are identified system parameters;

, Eq.(5) can be further expressed as follows:

The procedure for implementing the RLS method with the forgetting [31] factor λ is presented in the following form:
(9)

)
,
where
θ ^
k
,
K k
are the estimated state vector and the updated gain at time k , respectively; I is the identity matrix; and
P k
is the error covariance matrix.

In the context of complex nonlinear systems like battery systems, EKF and UKF have been widely studied and applied. However, these methods face certain challenges: EKF is susceptible to linearization errors, and estimating the Jacobian matrix is often difficult, while UKF assumes a constant covariance, which limits its ability to capture real-time dynamic noise characteristics. To address these issues, this paper introduces the mAUKF. The subsequent sections outline the implementation of this enhanced algorithm.

To apply KF algorithms, it is essential to first establish the state equation and the measurement equation because these equations form the mathematical foundation for predicting and updating the system states. The state equation models the dynamic behavior of the battery over time, while the measurement equation relates the observable data to the internal system states. Discretizing Eqs. (1) and (2) with the sample time
T s
, it yields
(10)
{
U
O C , k
−
U
T , k
=
U
1 , k
+
U
2 , k
+
R 0

B , k
,
U
1 , k
=
m 1
.
U
1 , k − 1
+
n 1
.
I
B , k
,
U
2 , k
=
m 2
.
U
2 , k − 1
+
n 2
.
I
B , k
.

In which the constant coefficients
m 1
,
m 2
,
n 1
, and
n 2
are defined as follows:
(11)
{
m 1
=
e
−
T s

R 1

1 −

)
,

,
n 2
=

)
.

Assume that
U 1
,
U 2
,
I B
remain constant during a sampling interval. From the above implementation, the parameters of the battery model (
R 0
,
R 1
,
R 2
,
C 1
,
C 2
) are determined using the FFRLS algorithm. Particularly, the open circuit voltage
U
O C
in Eq. (10) is determined from the relationship function between
U
O C
and
S O C
discussed in the next section.

In addition, during the process of determining the parameters of ECM, the state of charge (SOC) of the battery is calculated using the coulomb counting method as follows:
(12)
S O
C k
= S O
C

−

,
where
E n
is the battery normal capacity;
S O

k ,

, respectively.

Using Eqs. (10) and (12), the state equation describing ECM and involving SOC estimation is established as follows:
(13)
{

)
+
q

)

,

x k
is the state vector, i.e.,

;

;

;

T , k
is the terminal voltage of the battery;
f (

)
is the state function;
h (

,

B , k
)
is the nonlinear observation function;
q
k − 1
and
r k
are the process and measurement noise vectors which have covariance matrices
Q

and
R k
, respectively.

In which the state and observation functions are represented as follows:
(14)

R 0
.
,

Based on the state equation of ECM, the overall diagram describes the UKF algorithm as shown in Fig. 2 , with the definition of quantities presented in Table 2 . The UKF algorithm consists of three main stages: Initialization, Prediction, and Update. First, the initial state estimate
x ^
0
​ and the covariance matrix
P 0
​ are initialized. In the Prediction stage, sigma points are generated based on the current state estimate and covariance matrix. These sigma points are then propagated through the nonlinear system function to predict the next state estimate
x ^
k −
and the predicted covariance matrix
P k −
​. In the Update stage, the sigma points are mapped through the measurement function to compute the predicted measurements. The Kalman gain
K k
​ is then calculated based on the difference (or innovation) between the actual measurements and the predicted measurements. Finally, the state estimate and covariance matrix are updated accordingly. This process is repeated at each time step, allowing the UKF to continuously improve the state estimation accuracy of the system, particularly in the presence of nonlinearities.

Inspired by UKF, the AUKF modifies the noise measurement and process noise covariance [32] through the following:
(15)
{
ε
k
=
u k
− h
(
x ^
k
,
I
B , k
)
,
C k
=
1 M
.
∑
i = k − M + 1
k
ε
i
.
ε
i T
,
Q k
=
K k
.
C k
.
K k T
,
R k
=
C k
+
∑
i = 0
2 L
W i c
.
(
u
k | k − 1
i
−
u k
+
ε
k
)
.
(
u
k | k − 1
i
−
u k
+
ε
k
)
T
,
where
ε
k
is the voltage residual of the battery model at time step k;
C k
is the covariance matrix that characterizes the variability of
ε
k
over a window of size M .

Moreover, when estimating the time-varying process noise covariance matrix, it is crucial to prioritize the latest information over older data. To achieve this end, the paper proposes an exponential weighting method for estimating time-varying process noise covariance matrix. This method improves the accuracy of the process noise covariance matrix
Q k
​ estimation by increasing the influence of the most recent data.

To highlight the latest process noise covariance matrix, the exponential weighting method is applied, modifying the weights for each calculated matrix in the summation. The weight coefficients {
ϑ s
} are chosen to satisfy
ϑ s
=
ϑ
s − 1
b ,
b ∈
(
0 , 1
]
,
and
∑
s = 0
k
ϑ s
= 1
. In which b is the forgotten coefficient to adjust the adaptive sensitivity in real time.

This approach ensures that recent data points have higher weights, thus enhancing the responsiveness of the filter to changes in the process noise. The weight coefficients can be computed using the following formula:
(16)
ϑ s
=
d k
b
s
,
d k
=
1 − b
1 −
b
k + 1
,
s ∈
[
0 , k
)
.

By applying the exponential weighting method based on Eq. (16), the recursive formula for estimating the process noise covariance matrix
Q k
becomes
(17)
Q k
=
(
1 −
d k
)
.
Q
k − 1
+
d k
.
[
K k
.
C k
.
K k T
]
.

When the forgetting factor b is large (close to 1), more weight is given to the previously sampled data, causing the system to respond more slowly to new noise or changes. Conversely, when b is small, the weight of the next sampled data increases significantly, allowing the system to respond quickly to changes in the process noise. The weighting factor
d k
​ ensures that the sum of the weights during the update process remains equal to 1, maintaining a balance between the next and previous states. As k (time step) increases, the weight
d k
​ decreases exponentially because
b
s
diminishes. Consequently, the contribution of the next sampled data to the process noise covariance matrix
Q k
​ gradually reduces, while the influence of previous data
Q
k − 1
​ becomes dominant. At the initial stages, when k is small, the system is sensitive to new noise and reacts quickly to changes. However, as the system progresses to a stable state with a larger k , the covariance matrix
Q k
​ primarily relies on historical data. This behavior helps to suppress excessive oscillations and ensures the system maintains stability over time.

The exponential weighting method ensures that the most recent information has a greater impact on the estimation of the time-varying process noise covariance matrix
Q k
​. This approach improves the filter's ability to adapt to system changes, thereby enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the state estimation.

The combined approach of integrating the FFRLS and modify AUKF methods enables the online identification of model parameters and the accurate estimation of
S O C
. This process is illustrated in Fig. 3 . Initially, model parameters such as
R 0
,
R 1
,
C 1
and
R 2
,
C 2
are initialized based on prior knowledge. Given that the changes in these parameters between consecutive time steps are typically minimal. It is assumed that the model parameters remain constant from the time step
k − 1
to the time step k . Using the model parameters obtained at time step
k − 1
, the modified AUKF is employed to estimate the SOC at time step k . Subsequently, a one-to-one relationship between
U
O C
and
S O C
can be established by using the hybrid pulse power characterization method. This relationship allows for the calculation of
U
O C
at time step k , which is then fed into the FFRLS module to determine the model parameters at time step k . These parameters are then used to estimate the
S O C
at time step
k + 1
. The paper emphasizes that the variation in
U
O C
between consecutive time steps is not overlooked, and
S O C
estimation is consistently based on the model parameters identified in the previous time step. Through the iterative interaction of these two modules, the algorithm is capable of recursively updating and optimizing the state estimates and model parameters.
The pseudo-code of the SOC estimation algorithm with the modified AUKF is shown in Table 3

A Simulink model, depicted in Fig. 4 , demonstrates the implementation of the proposed battery model estimation method, using m-script with a 10-millisecond sampling time to implement the SOC estimation procedure for microcontroller-based applications. The lithium battery model, based on the A123 AMP20M1HD cell, is developed using the Simscape framework and incorporates parameters derived from the actual battery. A constant current-constant voltage (CCCV) control block manages the charging and discharging processes, ensuring safe operation of the battery by regulating current and voltage based on the state of the cell. The core of the model is the SOC estimation algorithm, which receives inputs of battery current and terminal voltage to compute the
S O C
, a critical metric for battery management systems.

The experimental setup, as illustrated in Fig. 5 , is designed to validate the proposed method for battery state estimation while carefully addressing hardware noise, sensor calibration, and measurement accuracy. The system uses an A123 lithium-ion battery (
19.5
A h
) as the test object for charge and discharge experiments. A Raspberry Pi 4 executes the SOC estimation algorithm and acquires measurement data through a 24-bit ADC module, which digitizes analog signals with high resolution, and a shunt resistor for precise current measurements. However, hardware noise, such as signal interference and quantization errors, can impact the accuracy of the measurements. To mitigate these effects, the system incorporates comprehensive sensor calibration procedures. The PCS-1000 instrument, which offers high-accuracy measurements with an error margin of
± ( 0.02 %
of reading
+
0.02 %
of range), was used not only as a baseline reference for Coulomb counting but also to calibrate the ADC module and the shunt resistor. This ensures the sensors provide reliable and consistent readings across the full operating range.

Additionally, the DC electronic load maintains a stable discharge current, minimizing variations that could introduce additional disturbances into the measurements. Crucially, all hardware-induced noise is carefully recorded and incorporated into the simulation environment, ensuring that the simulation mirrors real-world conditions. This approach guarantees consistency between experimental data and the simulation model, enabling robust validation of the SOC estimation method under realistic noise scenarios.

Fig. 6 illustrates the schematic of the proposed HIL architecture for simulation of the estimation algorithm with dedicated hardware, which comprises three main components: the simulation environment (computer), the I/O server, and the execution hardware. Within the simulation environment, a battery model is developed in MATLAB Simulink to simulate the behavior of a Li-ion A123-AMP20M1HD battery. Key parameters of the battery model include a capacity of
19.5
A h
and a nominal voltage of
3.3
V
. The model simulates the charging and discharging processes of the battery with generated current signals, set at a discharge rate of
0.5 C
(
9.75
A
) and a variable current charge according to experimental requirements. An Ethernet connection establishes communication between the computer and an I/O server, facilitating the transfer of control signals and battery specifications. This setup effectively replaces the actual battery with the simulation model in practical scenarios. The next component, the I/O server, uses an ADAM acquisition module that connects directly to the computer via Ethernet and functions as a proxy for the real battery. The ADAM module receives control signals (charge/discharge current, terminal voltage, clock pulse) from the computer and converts digital signals into analog signals to emulate the battery's technical parameters. These parameters must be designed to correspond with the capabilities and ranges of real-world signals, such as battery voltage (
0
− −
3.3
V
) and shunt resistor signals (
75
m V / 10
A
). With flexible outputs, the I/O server can simulate various scenarios and ranges, not limited to just the A123 battery. The I/O server and simulation environment cooperate in order to generate a battery emulator that entirely replaces the actual load and battery. Besides, the execution hardware mirrors the experimental setup, including a data collection module (ADC module) and Raspberry Pi for microcontroller. The ADC module gathers analog signals from the battery emulator and converts them into digital data for the Raspberry Pi to process. The Raspberry Pi acts as the central processing unit, executing battery management algorithms such as FFRLS, AUKF, or UKF to estimate the real-time SOC of the battery. The results from these algorithms are sent back to the I/O server and MATLAB model for analysis and performance evaluation. The testing results are compared to simulation data gathered from the synchronized simulation environment with clock pulses, and the algorithm's performance is evaluated.

Fig. 7 illustrates the experimental setup established in the laboratory, comprising the following main components: a laptop, a direct current (DC) power supply, an I/O Server (ADAM 6024), and execution hardware included Raspberry Pi 4 and 24-bit ADC module. The sensors and primary microcontroller are configured in line with the experimental model that is being shown; however, a battery emulator constructed from an I/O server and computer simulation has been used in place of the battery, load, shunt resistor, and voltage/current observe components. This allows for hardware-bound algorithm evaluation without requiring for a real-world environment, which has advantages in terms of simplicity of deployment, cost savings, effort savings, and testing safety. This process ensures that the hardware performance such as signal acquisition, computation delays, and noise filtering is accurately represented during testing. As a result, the HIL-based evaluation provides a robust platform to verify whether the proposed algorithm can be successfully deployed on the actual hardware while maintaining accuracy and reliability.

Firstly, the root-mean-square-error (RMSE) and the mean-absolute-error (MAE) are used to compare the estimation algorithms. The MSE and MAE are computed according to the following formulas
(18)
R M S E =
∑ i K
(
y i
−
y ^
i
)
2
K
,
(19)
M A E =
1 K
∑
i = 1
K
|
y i
−
y ^
i
|
,
where
y ^
i
and
y i
are the measured and referenced values, and K is the number of samples.

The scenarios involve applying the commonly used algorithms UKF, AUKF, and the proposed algorithm mAUKF to the battery model in the Simulink environment. The simulation model is initialized with the parameters presented in Table 4 .

The FFRLS algorithm is implemented with a forgetting coefficient
λ F
= 0.9985
, which prioritizes retaining more historical information. This choice stabilizes the model over time but reduces its ability to adapt quickly to abrupt changes. Such a setting is suitable for battery models, which are inherently stable systems with parameters that change slowly over time. The initial variance matrix
P
0 , F
= 1 e − 8 * e y e
( 5 )
, where
e y e ( 5 )
is a
5
×
5
matrix with diagonal elements equal to 1, reflects a very low level of initial uncertainty in the parameters. This small variance ensures slow changes at the beginning, stabilizing the system as the Kalman Filter coefficients are not yet converged. Without such stabilization, the FFRLS algorithm would struggle to achieve convergence.

For the UKF, AUKF, and mAUKF algorithms, the filter parameters are set as
α = 0.01 ;
σ = 1 ;
β = 2 ; L = 3
. The small value of
α = 0.01
results in a narrow distribution of sigma points centered on the mean value, making it well-suited for systems with mild nonlinearity. The parameter
β = 2
is optimal for Gaussian distributions, enhancing the filter's performance in systems where noise follows a normal distribution. The parameter
L = 3
indicates that the state vector in the model has three variables, namely
U 1
,
U 2
and
S O C
. In scenarios involving stronger nonlinearity or significant noise,
σ = 1
is applied to ensure that the sigma points cover the entire state space more effectively.

The initial SOC is set to
S O
C 0
= 1
during discharge and
S O
C 0
= 0.01
during charge, aligning with the boundary conditions of the battery operation. The measurement noise covariance matrix
R 0
= 0.1
and the process noise covariance matrix
Q 0
= diag
(
[
1 e − 5 ,
1 e − 5 ,
1 e − 5
]
)
, where
d i a g ( * )
is the diagonal of a
3
×
3
matrix, represent the low noise levels assumed in the system. The small values in
Q 0
​ indicate confidence in the model's accuracy and reflect low state noise, ensuring smoother parameter updates.

These parameter choices, including the stabilization of FFRLS, fine-tuned sigma point distribution in UKF-based methods, and low noise assumptions, have been made to ensure that the algorithms achieve both robustness and high accuracy. Specific case studies and further explanations of these parameters are provided in their respective sections for clarity and context.

With the simulation parameters presented, the estimated output voltage y from FFRLS, the estimated terminal voltage
U T
​ from KF, and the estimated
S O C
are presented in Fig. 8 . The findings indicate that each stage of the estimated methods performs effectively as illustrated by the error curves. Furthermore, both AUKF and mAUKF significantly outperform the UKF, as evidenced by the error data in Table 5 .

In the absence of
S O C 0
error, Fig. 8c demonstrates that the difference between the AUKF and mAUKF algorithms is negligible, with AUKF showing a slight advantage in error estimation. However, when the
S O C 0
error increases to
20 %
in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10
, the performance of the model becomes dependent on initial conditions, such as the value of
P o
. Figs. 9a, 9b, 10a, and 10b correspond to pure simulation scenarios 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, providing insights into how different initialization strategies impact the estimated convergence.

Specifically, if
P o
is underestimated (i.e., the initial covariance is small), the AUKF algorithm tends to rely heavily on the initial value, resulting in slow convergence to the true value, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (scenarios 1 and 2). In contrast, the mAUKF algorithm, owing to its improvements in the computational approach, can adjust and achieve faster convergence under such conditions. In Scenario 1, the mAUKF algorithm requires approximately 800 s to achieve an RMSE of less than
0.1
(a reduction of 80 % from its initial value), compared to 1458 s for the AUKF, representing an improvement of over
45 %
in convergence time. In Scenario 2, the mAUKF demonstrates an even faster convergence rate, reaching an RMSE of <0.04 (also a reduction of 80 % from the initial value) within 100 s, compared to 298 s for the AUKF, resulting in an improvement of over 60 %.

On the other hand, when
P o
is well-initialized, as shown in Fig. 10 (scenarios 3 and 4), the performance of both AUKF and mAUKF is significantly enhanced, with AUKF regaining its advantage in SOC estimation. This is reflected in the superior RMSE and MAE values, presented in Table 6 . Therefore, it can be concluded that in unstable battery systems, where estimation errors are prevalent, the mAUKF algorithm offers better convergence performance. A practical example of such a system would be a disconnection line between the BMS and the battery, where the uncertainty of
S O C 0
upon reconnection poses a critical challenge, one that the mAUKF algorithm is specifically designed to address.

Using the experimental model depicted in Fig. 5, a single lithium battery cell (A123) was discharged with a current of
0.5 C
, equivalent to
9.75 A
. The experiment was conducted in two phases: first, determining the SOCOCV relationship using the HPPC method, as illustrated in Fig. 11 ; and second, continuously discharging the cell at a fixed current to test the SOC estimation algorithm. Fig. 11 outlines the process of determining the SOCOCV relationship, where data was acquired using a reference device to ensure high accuracy. Based on the experimental results, the SOCOCV relationship was determined to be represented by an 8th-order polynomial function:
(20)
U
O C
= − 129
× S O
C
8
+ 654
× S O
C
7
− 1345
× S O
C
6
+ 1464
× S O
C
5
− 920
× S O
C
4
+ 341
× S O
C
3
− 73
× S O
C
2
+ 8.97
× S O
C
1
+ 2.8

The results of the continuous discharge of the battery cell are presented in Fig. 12 , in which the blue and green line represent the data collected from the sensor, and the red line corresponds to the reference data. The sampling time for measurement is 1 s, matching the sampling interval used in the simulation, while the reference device operates at a higher sampling rate of 0.1 s to ensure accurate calculations, such as coulomb counting. The measurement error, compared to the reference data, is quantified as
± 15
m A
for current and
± 5
m V
for voltage.

Subsequently, the algorithms are experimentally applied during the discharge process using three different methods. The initial error is set to
20 %
, consistent with the settings described in the simulation. Figs. 13 and 14
correspond to experimental scenarios 5 and 6, respectively, demonstrating the impact of different initialization strategies on algorithm performance.

In Fig. 13 (scenario 5), the algorithms operate under conditions of low initial covariance, where the model relies heavily on its initial estimate. In this scenario, the mAUKF algorithm demonstrates superior convergence performance compared to the AUKF, achieving lower RMSE and MAE values as listed in Table 7 . The results suggest that the adaptive mechanism in mAUKF allows it to adjust more effectively under tighter initial conditions, preventing large deviations from the true state. In this scenario, the mAUKF algorithm requires approximately 824 s to achieve an RMSE of <0.04 (a reduction of 80 % from its initial value), compared to 1617 s for the AUKF, representing an improvement of just over 49 % in convergence time.

Fig. 14 (scenario 6) examines the case of higher initial covariance, allowing the algorithms more flexibility to adjust their state estimates during the discharge process. Under these conditions, both the AUKF and mAUKF algorithms show improved performance. Meanwhile, the UKF algorithm consistently produces the largest error in both scenarios, emphasizing the importance of adaptive approaches when dealing with initial uncertainties.

Using the battery data from the Simscape model in the simulation and the hardware described in the system description section, the HIL architecture was implemented, and the results are shown in Fig. 15 . The experiment shows that the current measurement error is
± 25
m A
, while the voltage measurement error is
± 5
m V
. When compared to the actual data, the error in the HIL simulation is larger than that observed in the experiment. This discrepancy can be attributed to the accumulation of errors from the same sensor, compounded by the additional ADC output noise from the server I/O block, which amplifies the noise. Another noticeable difference is the discrepancy in the voltage profile between the actual battery and the simulation, which can be explained by variations in the intrinsic properties of individual battery cells, as each battery block possesses distinct electrical properties.

Two charging and discharging scenarios, each with a 20 % initialization error, were conducted to further assess the effectiveness of the algorithm under varying initial covariance values, as illustrated in Figs. 16 and 17
. Figs. 16a, 16b, 17a, and 17b correspond to experimental scenarios 7, 8, 9, and 10, respectively, providing deeper insights into the algorithm's performance across different charging and discharging cycles. A comparison of the HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop) results in Figs. 16 and 17 with the simulation results in Figs. 9 and 10 reveals a strong similarity, confirming the consistency of algorithm behavior in both settings. However, the HIL results exhibit larger errors, as indicated in Table 8 compared to Table 6, highlighting the impact of real-world noise and hardware limitations. Despite these challenges, the results demonstrate that the algorithm remains feasible for real-world implementation using the sensors and microprocessors applied in this study.

Furthermore, when examining Fig. 13 and Fig. 14 from the experimental discharge process, a significant discrepancy can be observed compared to the corresponding Fig. 16a and Fig. 17a. This difference arises from the variation between the actual and simulated battery models, where determining the initialization parameters for the actual battery model poses a substantial challenge. Despite these differences, it is important to note that the relative performance of the algorithms remains consistent, with the UKF yielding the highest error. In contrast, the mAUKF demonstrates significant improvement, particularly when the initial error is large, and the initial covariance is not accurately predicted, as observed specifically in Scenario 7.

The proposed algorithm, validated on a single-cell setup, demonstrates significant potential for application in large-scale battery packs, such as those used in electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage systems (ESS). However, when transitioning to larger systems, additional requirements arise. First, a battery balancing system becomes essential to manage cell-to-cell imbalances caused by differences in capacity, temperature, and internal resistance. Such systems, whether active or passive, are crucial for maintaining uniform performance across all cells and extending pack lifespan. Second, comprehensive monitoring and protection systems are required to track voltage, current, and temperature for each cell, ensuring timely detection of issues like over-voltage, under-voltage, and thermal runaway. These systems must include fault detection and protection mechanisms to guarantee operational safety. Lastly, large-scale systems demand increased computational efficiency and optimized communication to process data from multiple cells in real time. Future work will focus on addressing these challenges by integrating the proposed algorithm into a complete BMS framework. To confirm its feasibility, a preliminary simulation on a 29-cell battery pack was conducted in scenario 11. The results affirm that the algorithm maintains high SOC estimation accuracy and computational efficiency, proving its scalability. The results shows in

Table 9 indicate that the improvement achieved by the mAUKF in the 29-cell battery block is consistent with its performance in the 1-cell battery model, demonstrating the algorithm's scalability and effectiveness across different system sizes.
Fig. 18 , Fig. 19 , Fig. 20

This study introduces a practical approach to estimating the SOC of batteries by integrating the mAUKF algorithm with a HIL simulation platform. The proposed method not only improves estimation accuracy but also ensures practical implementation that aligns with the requirements of modern battery management systems and battery technologies. This integration ensures that the proposed method is both reliable and applicable to experimental and real-world commercial scenarios. The study offers an extensive evaluation of the proposed mAUKF algorithm compared to the traditional AUKF through experimental and simulation data, including charge and discharge scenarios. By presenting specific quantitative metrics, such as convergence time and RMSE reduction, the research clearly demonstrates the advantages of the proposed method, highlighting its technical reliability and practical benefits.

The advanced HIL platform plays an important role in validating battery management algorithms. It combines simulation with real hardware, providing a flexible and cost-effective environment for testing innovative methods without requiring immediate deployment on physical systems. This not only reduces costs and safety risks but also shortens the testing process. In addition, the platform ensures efficient evaluation by allowing performance testing of microcontrollers and the impact of sensor noise under realistic conditions. This help optimize algorithms in real-world applications.

The results confirm the mAUKF algorithm's superiority over the traditional AUKF. Specifically, in scenarios with large and unpredictable initial errors, mAUKF reduces RMSE by 80 % within 824 s, which is 49 % faster than AUKF (1617 s). In discharge simulations, mAUKF reduces RMSE to below 0.1 within 800 s, a 45 % improvement over AUKF (1458 s). Similarly, in charge simulations, mAUKF achieves the same RMSE reduction in only 100 s, a 60 % improvement compared to AUKF (298 s). These results demonstrate the mAUKF's ability to handle large errors and uncertain conditions effectively, improving both its convergence rate and accuracy.

In conclusion, the utilization of the mAUKF algorithm and the HIL platform provides a robust solution for SOC estimation. The proposed approach enhances estimation accuracy, convergence rate, and simplifies the testing process, making it both reliable and cost-effective for practical use. This work offers significant advancements in battery management and energy storage systems, creating a solid foundation for future innovations in the field.

Quoc Dan Le: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation. Quoc Minh Lam: Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Minh Nhat Huynh: Validation, Resources. Huy Hung Nguyen: Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Van Tu Duong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is funded by Vietnam National University Ho Chi Minh City (VNU-HCM) under grant number B2023–20–02. We acknowledge the support of time and facilities from Key Laboratory of Digital Control and System Engineering (DCSELab), Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology (HCMUT), VNU-HCM for this study.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy Storage technologies: An integrated Survey of developments, Global Economical/Environmental effects, Optimal Scheduling model, and Sustainable Adaption Policies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Amir et al., “Energy Storage technologies: An integrated Survey of developments, Global Economical/Environmental effects, Optimal Scheduling model, and Sustainable Adaption Policies,” Nov. 30, 2023, Elsevier Ltd. doi: 10.1016/j.est.2023.108694.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Parameter estimation of vehicle batteries in V2G systems: an exogenous function-based approach
IEEE Trans. Ind. Electron.

Yu, “Parameter estimation of vehicle batteries in V2G systems: an exogenous function-based approach,” IEEE Trans. Ind. Electron., vol. 69, no. 9, pp. 9535–9546, Sep. 2022, doi: 10.1109/TIE.2021.3112980.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bidirectional charging in V2G systems: an in-cell variation analysis of vehicle batteries
IEEE Syst J

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.M. Khalid and J.C.H. Peng, “Bidirectional charging in V2G systems: an in-cell variation analysis of vehicle batteries,” IEEE Syst J, vol. 14, no. 3, pp. 3665–3675, Sep. 2020, doi: 10.1109/JSYST.2019.2958967.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A closer look at State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) estimation techniques for batteries
Analog devises

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Murnane and A. Ghazel, “A closer look at State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) estimation techniques for batteries,” Analog devises, 2024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium polymer battery state-of-charge estimation based on adaptive unscented kalman filter and support vector machine
IEEE Trans. Power Electron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Meng, G. Luo, and F. Gao, “Lithium polymer battery state-of-charge estimation based on adaptive unscented kalman filter and support vector machine,” IEEE Trans. Power Electron, vol. 31, no. 3, pp. 2226–2238, Mar. 2016, doi: 10.1109/TPEL.2015.2439578.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State of charge estimation of lithium-ion batteries based on online OCV curve construction
Batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, R. Gong, Z. Yang, and L. Kang, “State of charge estimation of lithium-ion batteries based on online OCV curve construction,” Batteries, vol. 10, no. 6, pp. 1–15, Jun. 2024, doi: 10.3390/batteries10060208.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The state of charge estimating methods for battery: a review
ISRN Appl. Math.
